bibliographic_data.title: Optimizing the Training of Co-Located Deep Learning Models Using Cache-Aware Staggering
bibliographic_data.abstract: Despite significant advances, training deep learning models remains a time-consuming and resource-intensive task. One of the key challenges in this context is the ingestion of the training data, which involves non-trivial overheads: read the training data from a remote repository, apply augmentations and transformations, shuffle the training samples, and assemble them into mini-batches. Despite the introduction of abstractions such as data pipelines that aim to hide such overheads asynchronously, it is often the case that the data ingestion is slower than the training, causing a delay at each training iteration. This problem is further augmented when training multiple deep learning models simultaneously on powerful compute nodes that feature multiple GPUs. In this paper, we study the problem of how to minimize the makespan of running two training instances that reuse the same training data. The makespan is subject to a trade-off: if the training instances start at the same time, competition for I/O bandwidth slows down the data pipelines and increases the makespan. If one training instance is staggered, competition is reduced but the makespan increases. We aim to optimize this trade-off by proposing a performance model capable of predicting the makespan based on the staggering between the training instances, which can be used to find the optimal staggering that triggers just enough competition to make optimal use of transparent caching in order to minimize the makespan. Experiments with different combinations of learning models using the same training data demonstrate that (1) staggering is important to minimize the makespan; (2) our performance model is accurate and can predict the optimal staggering in advance based on calibration overhead.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a performance model for optimizing the training of co-located deep learning models using cache-aware staggering to minimize makespan.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors propose a performance model that predicts the optimal staggering between training instances to reduce competition for I/O bandwidth and optimize caching usage, thereby minimizing the overall makespan.
outcomes: Staggering is important to minimize the makespan of running two training instances that reuse the same training data.
The proposed performance model can accurately predict the optimal staggering based on calibration overhead.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Despite significant advances, training deep learning models remains a time-consuming and resource-intensive task. One of the key challenges is the ingestion of training data, which involves non-trivial overheads such as reading from remote repositories, applying augmentations, shuffling samples, and assembling mini-batches. Data pipelines aim to hide these overheads but often fail to keep up with training steps due to high I/O latencies and insufficient bandwidth. This issue is exacerbated in scenarios where multiple models are trained simultaneously on powerful compute nodes with limited I/O bandwidth. The authors propose a performance model that predicts the optimal staggering between training instances to minimize makespan by balancing competition for I/O bandwidth and caching usage.
